tokens: Houston, how do you read Columbia on the high gain?
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: Houston, Columbia went Up Telemetry Command, Reset to re-acquire on the high gain.
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: And do you want V horizontal 5515 decimal 2?
transcript.speaker: Aldrin

tokens: Like an AGS to PGNS align?
transcript.speaker: Aldrin

tokens: Hey, Houston, that may have seemed like a very long final phase.

The Auto targeting was taking us right into a football-field-sized crater, with a large number of big boulders and rocks for about one or two crater diameters around it, and it required us going in P66 and flying manually over the rock field to find a reasonably good area.
transcript.speaker: Armstrong

tokens: It was beautiful from here, Tranquility.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We'll get to the details of what's around here (later), but it looks like a collection of just about every variety of shape, angularity, granularity, about every variety of rock you could find.
The color is...Well, it varies pretty much depending on how you're looking relative to the zero-phase point.
There doesn't appear to be too much of a general color at all.
However, it looks as though some of the rocks and boulders, of which there are quite a few in the near area...
It looks as though they're going to have some interesting colors to them.
transcript.speaker: Aldrin

tokens: We'll let you press on through the simulated countdown, and we'll talk to you later.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: This one-sixth g is just like the airplane.
transcript.speaker: Aldrin

tokens: Be advised there're lots of smiling faces in this room and all over the world.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Well, there are two of them up here.
transcript.speaker: Armstrong

tokens: That was a beautiful job, you guys.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: And don't forget one (smiling face) in the command module.
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: We have you pitched up about 4-1/2 degrees.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: That's confirmed by our local observation.
transcript.speaker: Armstrong

tokens: And thanks for putting me on relay, Houston.
I was missing all the action.
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: We'll enable MSFN (Manned Spaceflight Network) relay.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I just got it, I think.
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: They (the LM crew) ought to be able to hear you.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Tranquility Base, it sure sounded great from up here.
You guys did a fantastic job.
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: Just keep that orbiting base ready for us up there now.
transcript.speaker: Armstrong

tokens: LOS (Loss of Signal) 103:27; AOS (Acquisition of Signal) 104:13 .
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: You're looking good in every respect.
We copy the DPS (Descent Propulsion System) venting.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Houston, the guys that said that we wouldn't be able to tell precisely where we are, are the winners today.

We were a little busy worrying about program alarms and things like that in the part of the descent where we would normally be picking out our landing spot; and aside from a good look at several of the craters we came over in the final descent, I haven't been able to pick out the things on the horizon as a reference as yet.
transcript.speaker: Armstrong

tokens: We'll figure out...We'll figure it out.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: You might be interested to know that I don't think we notice any difficulty at all in adapting to one-sixth g.
It seems immediately natural to move in this environment.
transcript.speaker: Armstrong

tokens: The area out the left-hand window is a relatively level plain cratered with a fairly large number of craters of the 5- to 50-foot variety; and some ridges (which are) small, 20, 30 feet high, I would guess; and literally thousands of little, 1- and 2-foot craters around the area.
We see some angular blocks out several hundred feet in front of us that are probably two feet in size and have angular edges.
There is a hill in view, just about on the ground track ahead of us.
Difficult to estimate, but might be a half a mile or a mile.
transcript.speaker: Armstrong

tokens: Sounds like it looks a lot better than it did yesterday...
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: It looked rough as a (corn) cob then.
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: Over the targeted landing area, it was extremely rough, cratered, and large numbers of rocks that were probably some - many - larger than 5 or 10 feet in size.
transcript.speaker: Armstrong

tokens: After you get through this P57, we'd like an E-memory dump.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: After this first P57, you want an E-memory dump.
transcript.speaker: Aldrin

tokens: We have a P22 update for you, if you're ready to copy.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: This is based on the targeted landing site.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Based on the targeted landing site; T1, 104:32:18; T2, 104:37:28, and 4 miles south.
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: Do you have any idea whether they landed left or right of center line?
Just a little bit long, is that all we know?
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: Apparently that's about all we can tell.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: And, Houston, our mission timer is now reading 902:34:47 and static.
transcript.speaker: Armstrong

tokens: Copy your mission timer's now static.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: That gravity align looked good to us.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I was trying to get time, (Verb)16 (Noun) 65, out, and somehow it proceeded on to the 06 22 before I could do a Verb 32 Enter.
I want to log a time here, and then I'd like to know whether you want me to proceed on the torquing angles or to go back and reenter again before torquing.
transcript.speaker: Aldrin

tokens: We'd like you to recall P57 and run through the gravity align one more time.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: For the mission timer, two suggestions.
Check the circuit breaker, panel 11; also, Reset and attempt to start.
That nine in the first digit might have something to do with it.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: If the circuit breaker is in, when I put it in Reset, I get 902:04:40.
When I release it now, I get 902:04:49.
I'm going to cycle the circuit breaker.
transcript.speaker: Armstrong

tokens: I cycled the circuit breaker and got all nines.
(Pause) And it will not now reset from all nines.
transcript.speaker: Armstrong

tokens: We'll research this problem and be back with you momentarily on the mission event time - correction - the mission timer.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I'd say the color of the local surface is very comparable to that we observed from orbit at this Sun angle - about 10 degrees Sun angle, or that nature.
It's gray; and it's a very white, chalky gray, as you look into the zero-phase line.
And it's considerably darker gray, more like ashen gray as you look out 90 degrees to the Sun.

Some of the surface rocks in close here that have been fractured or disturbed by the rocket engine plume are coated with this light gray on the outside; but where they've been broken, they display a very dark gray interior; and it looks like it could be country basalt.
transcript.speaker: Armstrong

tokens: Please vent fuel and Ox(idizer pressure) again.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: You can open both fuel and Ox vent now.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Houston, Tranquility standing by for Go on
transcript.speaker: Aldrin

tokens: You are Go for the AGS to PGNS align, and then the lunar align.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We show 30 psi in the fuel and 30 in the oxidizer (which are not unusual readings).
transcript.speaker: Armstrong

tokens: We're reading somewhat different than that.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: The fuel temperature is reading 64 in the descent, and the oxidizer...(Clarifying what he has just said) That's descent (tank) 2.
And the oxidizer is off-scale low.
Descent 1 is showing 61 in the fuel and 65 in the oxidizer.
transcript.speaker: Armstrong

tokens: Please take the fuel vent switch and hold it open.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Now indicating 20 psi on the fuel side.
transcript.speaker: Armstrong

tokens: Now indicating 15 psi in both tanks.
transcript.speaker: Armstrong

tokens: If you haven't done so, you can release the fuel vent switch now.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We have an indication that we've frozen up the descent-fuel helium heat exchanger - and with some fuel trapped in the line between there and the valves...And the pressure we're looking at is increasing there.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: If you have not done so, please close both Fuel and Ox vents now.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: From the surface, we could not see any stars out the window; but out my overhead hatch (means the overhead rendezvous window), I'm looking at the Earth.
It's big and bright and beautiful.
Buzz is going to give a try at seeing some stars through the optics.
transcript.speaker: Armstrong

tokens: You're looking great going over the hill.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Glad to hear the system's looking good.
You have a suggested attitude for me?
This one here seems all right.
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: Let me know when it's lunch time, will you?
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: You got a good attitude right there.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: If you want me to, I can give you a hack on the mission time, every 30 minutes.
I'm counting down to T3 time.
If you'd like to give me a hack, we can set up an event timer.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: What we're looking for, Charlie, is the time counting up to T2 that'll be equal to 60 minutes...(Correcting himself) Or, at T3 be equal to 60 minutes at T3.
transcript.speaker: Armstrong

tokens: Reset the event timer to zero.
And, on my Mark at 103:39:41, we'll give you a hack, and it'll be 1 hour (prior to T3).
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: And we've got almost 3 minutes to go, Neil.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Tranquility Base, stand by on the event timer.
On my Mark start your event timer.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We'd like you to torque that.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Do you want us to accept this position?
transcript.speaker: Aldrin

tokens: We'd like you to pull the circuit breaker on panel 11 for the mission timer.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We'd like you to reject that RLS (Reference Landing Site).
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We'd like you to call...After this, call P00 and give us a E(rasable)-memory dump.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Did I copy Program Alarm 1106 from you?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Could that, by any chance, be due to the fact that I flashed the updata link switch to Data while that was going on?
transcript.speaker: Aldrin

tokens: The SPAN guys think that's conceivable.
I think we want another Verb 74.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: On my Mark it will be GET (Ground Elapsed Time) 103:53
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We have the LM ascent Pad.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Ready to copy the LM ascent Pad.
transcript.speaker: Aldrin

tokens: We also have a CSI Pad if you are ready to copy.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Coming at you with a CSI: Noun 11, 105:35:3700; 107:11:3000, 0538, minus all zeros.
FDAI is NA; 0937...Correction, 09356; 10315; plus 0538, minus all zeros, plus 0012.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Rl is plus 0538, and we have a load for you.
Will you please give us P00 and Data?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Before I do that I would like to designate the
transcript.speaker: Aldrin

tokens: Noun 86, plus 0538, plus all zeros, and the last one was 0012.
And what's the sign of that, please?
transcript.speaker: Armstrong

tokens: The Delta-VY is minus all zeros.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: On my Mark it will be 37 minutes to T3.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Does somebody down there have a mike button keyed?
transcript.speaker: Aldrin

tokens: Do you still hear it now?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: It sounds like somebody's banging some chairs around in the back room.
transcript.speaker: Aldrin

tokens: That's a VOGAA that you hear for the CSM to keep the noise down on the loop.
Maybe we got a MSFN relay or something.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We got the MSFN relay in.
You're hearing the VOGAA, which is a noise suppression device.
We'll try to take it out.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: It ought to be a little quieter up there now.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I think the noise has stopped now.
transcript.speaker: Aldrin

tokens: Mike, be advised we have an update for you on the P22 for the LM.
We estimate he landed about 4 miles downrange.
Your T1 times are updated and your T2, if you're ready to copy.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: You said 4 miles long, is that correct, Houston?
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: Houston, do you have an updated LM weight for us?
transcript.speaker: Aldrin

tokens: We'll have you a map location momentarily.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Do you have an updated LM weight for us?
transcript.speaker: Aldrin

tokens: Stand by on the DAP (Digital Auto Pilot).
Our DAP Pad for you is LM weight 10906 (terrestrial pounds).
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: With a latitude (and) longitude-over-two update for LM position.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We got an update on the lat(itude)/longitude for the LM, if you're ready to copy.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Columbia, it's plus 7 - correction - plus 0.799 for the Lat, plus 11.730 for the longitude-over-two.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: You are stay for a T3.
We have some surface block data if you're ready to copy.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Go ahead with your block data.
transcript.speaker: Armstrong

tokens: We will not come up on the MSFN relay.
We'd like you to come on (control) panel 9 (and) turn on your VHF to TR.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: You want me to transmit for some reason with it?
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: You want me to transmit with the VHF for some reason?
Why do you want me in TR?
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: We don't want you to transmit, Mike.
We just want you in that position in case you want to talk to Tranquility.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I have a fairly good-sized difference between battery volts on (batteries) 5 and 6; 6 is reading 33.5 and 5 is reading 36.5.
transcript.speaker: Aldrin

tokens: They are both coming up in voltage.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Could you please give us a readout now of all of your descent tank pressures?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: On descent 1, fuel and oxidizer are reading 10 psi; and descent 2, fuel is reading 10 psi, oxidizer 11 psi.
transcript.speaker: Armstrong

tokens: Houston, Tranquility Base is ready to go through the powerdown and terminate the simulated countdown.
transcript.speaker: Aldrin

tokens: You can start your powerdown now.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: And, Tranquility Base, the White Team is going off now and letting the Maroon Team take over.
It was a beautiful job, you guys.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Couldn't have had better treatment from all of you back there.
transcript.speaker: Aldrin

tokens: Our recommendation at this point is planning an EVA, with your concurrence, starting about eight o'clock this evening, Houston time.
That is about 3 hours from now.
transcript.speaker: Armstrong

tokens: Well, we will give you some time to think about that.
transcript.speaker: Armstrong

tokens: We thought about it; we will support it.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: You guys are getting prime time TV there.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Hope that little TV set works, but we'll see.
transcript.speaker: Armstrong

tokens: Was your eight o'clock Houston time a reference to opening the hatch or starting the Prep for EVA at that time?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: It might be a little later than that, but in other words, start the Prep in about an hour or so.
transcript.speaker: Armstrong

tokens: We are ready to support you any time, Neil.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Stand by one, Charlie, for the next (garbled).
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: How did Tranquility look down there to you?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Well, the area looks smooth, but I was unable to see him.
I just picked out a distinguishable crater nearby and marked on it.
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: It looks like a nice area, though.
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: I understand you could not see Tranquility.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I say again, I could not see him.

Auto optics pointed at a spot very close to the coordinates which you gave me, so I picked a tiny crater in that area and marked on it so I will be able to have repeatable data, but I was unable to see him.
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: On our DPS venting and that fuel problem, our heat exchanger is cleared up.
It appears that the ice is melted, and we are in good shape now.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Tranquility is going to put the track mode to Slew now.
transcript.speaker: Aldrin

tokens: We noticed you are maneuvering very close to gimbal lock.
I suggest you move back away.
transcript.speaker: Garriott

tokens: I am going around it, doing a CMC Auto maneuver to the Pad values of roll 270, pitch 101, yaw 45.
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: (Faint, joking) How about sending me a fourth gimbal for Christmas.
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: First of all, we'd like a waste water dump to 10 percent on the backside.
Secondly, it does not look like we are going to need any plane change at this time, so we will not be uplinking a new REFSMMAT.
Third item, I would like all of your Cryo heaters to Auto, and we are ready for a battery charge, battery Bravo; it will last about 7 hours.
If you should go to sleep, we will be terminating that Batt charge, but at the moment, we can go ahead and start the Batt charge on Batt Bravo.
And a final item, for your SM RCS configuration for your rest period, register 1 for the DAP is 11111; DAP register 2, 01100.
And your AUTO RCS select switches, quad Alfa, pitch jets on only, quad Bravo all on, quad Charlie and quad Delta all off.
transcript.speaker: Garriott

tokens: Dump waste water to 10 percent on the backside.
Use prior REFSMMAT, CRYO heaters on to AUTO, battery B charge until I go to sleep.
Select quads A pitch only on, C and D all up.
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: We will have a state vector update for you a little later.
We are not prepared with it right now.
And on another subject, from Tranquility Base, they are prepared to begin the EVA early.
They expect to begin Depress operations in about 3 hours at 108...approximately 108 GET (Ground Elapsed Time).
transcript.speaker: Garriott

tokens: Tell them to eat some lunch before they go.
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: We'd like your PRD (Personal Radiation Dosimeter) readout, and we have double-checked your EM dump.
transcript.speaker: Garriott

tokens: Understand our E-memory dump was good.
transcript.speaker: Armstrong

tokens: Columbia, we would like for you to Re-acq(uire) with your high gain; attempt a manual lock-on.
transcript.speaker: Garriott

tokens: The LMP's (PRD) readout may possibly be 09017.
transcript.speaker: Aldrin

tokens: 09017 is an update on your readout.
transcript.speaker: Garriott

tokens: I'll let you know for sure when it goes to either 12 or 18.
transcript.speaker: Aldrin

tokens: The medics report your latter reading, "17", appears to be the correct one.
transcript.speaker: Garriott

tokens: Request P00 in Accept, and we'll uplink another state vector.
transcript.speaker: Garriott

tokens: Suggest you put Batt A on your Batt relay bus.
transcript.speaker: Garriott

tokens: We've reviewed the checklist, and about the only change in order to advance the EVA that we've found is that you'll want to delay your lithium hydroxide change until after the EVA rather than before.
transcript.speaker: Garriott

tokens: We'd just as soon make a change and jettison the old one.
transcript.speaker: Aldrin

tokens: We would like to delay that (primary) LiOH change until after the EVA.
There is a possibility you could jettison the canister when you jettison your PLSS.
transcript.speaker: Garriott

tokens: We show your EVAP OUT temperature running low.
Request you go to manual temperature control and bring it up.
You can check the procedures in ECS MAL 17.
transcript.speaker: Garriott

tokens: I have a P22 update for you.
transcript.speaker: Garriott

tokens: Your P22 AUTO - AUTO optics landmark ID on LM.
T1, 106 plus 30 plus 31; T2, 106 plus 35 plus 41, 2 nautical miles south.
transcript.speaker: Garriott

tokens: We have your LOS in 3 minutes.
transcript.speaker: Garriott

tokens: I'd like to take this opportunity to ask every person listening in, whoever and wherever they may be, to pause for a moment and contemplate the events of the past few hours and to give thanks in his or her own way.
transcript.speaker: Aldrin

tokens: That's about ready to fall off.
transcript.speaker: Aldrin

tokens: As a matter of fact, it just doesn't look like it sunk in at all.
transcript.speaker: Armstrong

tokens: We'd like some estimate of how far along you are with your eating and when you may be ready to start your EVA Prep.
transcript.speaker: Garriott

tokens: I think that we'll be ready to start EVA Prep in about a half hour or so.
transcript.speaker: Armstrong

tokens: We are beginning our EVA Prep.
transcript.speaker: Armstrong

tokens: Tranquility Base, this is Houston, Roger.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: The waste-water dump is down to 10 percent.
I have a question on the P22.
Do you want me to do another P22, or is all that information just for my own use in tracking the LM for photographic purposes?
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: We request that you perform another P22.
We'd like you to let the Auto optics take care of the tracking and devote your energies to trying to pick out the LM (visually) on the lunar surface.
If you can find the LM, of course.
We're looking for marks on it; but tracking of geographical features doesn't do us all that much good.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: And on the ECS system, whatever the problem was, it seems to have gone away without any changing of J52 sensors or anything like that.
My glycol evaporator outlet Temp is up above 50 now, and it's quite comfortable in the cockpit; so we'll talk more about that one later.
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: Did you shift into manual control, or did the problem resolve itself under Auto control?
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: The problem went away under Auto.
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: I did cycle out of Auto into Manual, back into Auto.
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: We need a second set of
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: (Guffaws over the "3/4") Tranquility, this is Houston.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: How do you read on Omni D, Dog?
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: We're reading you loud with background noise on Omni D.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: I'll stay on D here for a while.
I'm about to go into P22 (a tracking program which keeps the sextant pointed at the targeted spot on the ground while he passes overhead).
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: I'm coming up on my time for the first pass when I may be able to see the LM.
Do you have any topographical cues that might help me out here?
Auto optics is tracking between two craters.
One of them, as the LM sees it, would be long at 11 o'clock.
The other would be short and behind him at 5 o'clock.
(Pause) These are great big old craters (or) depressions.
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: The best we can do on topo features is to advise you to look to the west of the irregularly shaped crater, and then work on down to the southwest of it.
Another possibility is the southern rim of the southern of the two old-looking craters.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: I kept my eyes glued to the sextant that time hoping I'd get a flash of specular light off the LM, but I wasn't able to see any in my scan areas that you suggested.
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: On that southern of the old craters, there's a small bright crater on the southern rim.
One plot would put him slightly to the west of that small bright crater, about 500 to 1000 feet.
Do you see anything down there?
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: It's gone past now, Bruce, but I scanned that area that you are talking about very closely, and no, I did not see anything.
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: One of the craters I was talking about is located exactly at Mike 6.7 (on
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: The other one is located at 7...(Listens) The other one is located at 7.2, two-thirds of the way from Mike to Nan.
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: We believe you were looking a little too far to the west and south.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: I was looking where auto optics was tracking, on the average; and (I) understand that it should have been more to the north and more to the west; actually a tiny bit outside the circle, huh?
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: More to the north and a little more to the east.
The feature that I was describing to you - the small bright crater on the rim of the large, fairly old crater - would be about
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Well, just give me your best estimate as to his location in this coordinate system, and I'll plot it on my map and go from there.
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: Can you give us some idea of where you are in the surface checklist at the present time?
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: We're on the top of page
transcript.speaker: Aldrin

tokens: I finally got you back on Omni D.
I've been unsuccessfully trying to get you on the high gain, and I've gone Command Reset to Process.
How do you read me now?
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: Reading you loud with background noise.
Understand that's Omni Delta or Omni Bravo?
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Omni Delta and you were cut out.
I never got your coordinates on estimated LM position.
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: Estimated LM position is latitude plus 0.799, longitude-over-two plus 11.730.
On your chart we would place it...Stand by on the charts and (give me a) readback on the latitude and longitude.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: The latitude and longitude over two: 799 and 11730 are the ones that I('ve) been using in P22.
But what I'm interested in is grid coordinates on that map we're using.
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: We'll have them for you in a second.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Could you enable the S-band relay at least one-way from Eagle to Columbia so I can hear what's going on?
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: There's not much going on at the present time, Columbia.
I'll see what I can do about the relay.
Are you aware that Eagle plans the EVA about 4 hours early?
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: When's hatch open time in GET estimated?
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: We'll have an update for you on that a little later.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: I haven't heard a word from those guys, and I thought I'd be hearing them through your S-band relay.
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: They're on about page Surface 27 in the checklist, proceeding in good time.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: You got a crowd there in MCC (Mission Control Center)?
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: I'd expect you probably have about nine CapComs and eleven Flight Directors with no place to plug in (to the consoles).
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: (With a smile in his voice) Roger.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: That ratio might even be reversed.
(Long Pause) Glycol evaporator outlet temperature is 50 degrees (Fahrenheit, 10 C) and the comfort in here is just fine.
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: We copy 50 degrees on the glycol, and comfort index fine.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: And, if you'll excuse me a minute, I'm going to have a cup of coffee.
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: (Pause) Apollo...(Correcting himself) Columbia, this is Houston.
Your map coordinates (for the LM) are Papa decimal 2 and 6 decimal 3 on the
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: If you read, we request high-gain antenna yaw 180, pitch 0.
I say again, yaw 180, pitch 0 on the high gain.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Houston, Columbia on the high gain.
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: Columbia, this is Houston reading you loud and clear.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Reading you loud and clear, Bruce.
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: Well, what's new is I think we have some more coordinates for you on the LM location.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Six decimal three, I say again, six decimal three.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: What you are saying is, if you look at the Cat's Paw, then that's just about, oh, his middle finger; a little bit...one to two o'clock from his middle finger.
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: About one to two o'clock from the middle finger if you're using 12 o'clock being to the west.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: (Joking) That must be the way cats face (on the Moon).
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: And I got LOS and AOS times for you.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: The next pass for COAS tracking: your time of closest approach is 108:35:28.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: I understand all that, but with this new information would you like me to try P22 and look for him in a different spot?
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: Because I was looking in the wrong place last time.
Auto optics was not pointing me at the coordinates you gave.
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: On your next pass, Columbia, rather than performing a P22 as such, we would like you to look in the vicinity of the coordinates that we gave you, which is our best analysis based on math/physics and the trajectory.
And we also have another set of coordinates that we would like you to search in the vicinity of.
This last one being based on an interpretation of the geological features that were seen by the crew on their way down.
The coordinates of this second site are Mike 0.7 and 8.0.
I say again, Mike 0.7 and 8 (garbled) I say again, Mike 0.7 and 8.0.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: The only thing is, my best tool for looking is the sextant and if I'm going to crank the sextant up, I might as well let P22 go at the same time, or don't you think so?
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: If you want to go that way, crank it up, and then you can drive it around and look where you want.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: And if you can find the LM, then by all means, track it or make a note of where it was, and we can track it on the next Rev.
If you're ready, we have a
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: And this is an updated landing site REFSMMAT.
We still believe that a plane change is not required.
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: We are through with the uplink.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Roger, (on the) next pass, I'd appreciate the S-band relay mode.
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: There haven't been any transmissions from Tranquility Base since we last talked to you.
We cannot give you a full S-band relay without being assured of high-gain antenna.
We're working on the partial relay for you.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: All your systems are looking good from down here.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Does it look to you like the two-forty controller is properly controlling the glycol evaporator outlet Temp?
It looks all right up here.
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: During this pass on the front side, it looked okay to us.
transcript.speaker: McCandless
